topics.title: Definition and Classification
topics.content:
Diabetes Mellitus is a group of metabolic diseases characterized by hyperglycemia resulting from defects in insulin secretion, insulin action, or both. Chronic hyperglycemia is associated with long-term damage, dysfunction, and failure of various organs — especially the eyes, kidneys, nerves, heart, and blood vessels.

Classification
TypeMechanismKey Features
Type 1 DMAutoimmune β-cell destruction → absolute insulin deficiencyAutoantibodies (GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAA); typically younger onset but can occur at any age; requires insulin from diagnosis
Type 2 DMProgressive insulin secretory defect on background of insulin resistanceMost common form (~90-95%); associated with obesity, metabolic syndrome; often asymptomatic initially
GDMGlucose intolerance first recognized in pregnancyScreened at 24-28 weeks; increases risk of future T2DM
Specific typesMonogenic (MODY, neonatal DM), diseases of exocrine pancreas, drug-induced, endocrinopathiesSee monogenic diabetes section below
T1DM Staging
StageAutoantibodiesDysglycemiaSymptoms
Stage 1≥2 autoantibodiesNormoglycemiaNone
Stage 2≥2 autoantibodiesDysglycemia (IFG/IGT)None
Stage 3± autoantibodiesHyperglycemiaClinical diabetes present
Clinical Pearl: Stage 1 carries ~44% risk of progression to Stage 3 within 5 years, and ~85% lifetime risk. Teplizumab (anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody) is FDA-approved to delay Stage 3 onset in Stage 2 T1DM (age ≥8 years).

topics.title: Diagnostic Criteria
topics.content:
Diabetes Diagnosis

Any ONE of the following (must be confirmed on repeat testing unless unequivocal hyperglycemia):

TestDiagnostic Threshold
HbA1c≥6.5% (48 mmol/mol)
Fasting Plasma Glucose (FPG)≥126 mg/dL (7.0 mmol/L) — fasting ≥8 hours
2-hour OGTT≥200 mg/dL (11.1 mmol/L) — 75g glucose load
Random Plasma Glucose≥200 mg/dL (11.1 mmol/L) + classic symptoms
Important: If two different tests are available and BOTH are above threshold → diagnosis confirmed. If discordant → repeat the test that is above threshold.
Prediabetes Criteria
TestPrediabetes Range
HbA1c5.7–6.4% (39–47 mmol/mol)
IFG (Impaired Fasting Glucose)100–125 mg/dL (5.6–6.9 mmol/L)
IGT (Impaired Glucose Tolerance)140–199 mg/dL (7.8–11.0 mmol/L) on 2h OGTT
Conditions Affecting HbA1c Accuracy
  • Falsely low: Hemolytic anemias, hemoglobinopathies (HbS, HbC, HbF), chronic kidney disease, blood loss/transfusion, EPO therapy, iron deficiency anemia (variable — can be falsely high or low)
  • Falsely high: Iron deficiency (some assays), hypertriglyceridemia, hyperbilirubinemia, chronic alcoholism, splenectomy
  • Unreliable: Use FPG or OGTT instead when HbA1c is unreliable
AABBCC Classification Tool

A structured approach to classify difficult/atypical diabetes cases:

  • Autoantibodies (present/absent)
  • Age of onset
  • BMI
  • Beta-cell function (C-peptide)
  • Comorbidities (metabolic syndrome features)
  • Complications at presentation

topics.title: Screening Recommendations
topics.content:
Universal Screening
  • All adults: Begin at age 35, repeat every 3 years if normal
  • Earlier/more frequent if BMI ≥25 (≥23 in Asian Americans) PLUS one or more risk factors:
    • First-degree relative with diabetes
    • High-risk race/ethnicity (African American, Latino, Native American, Asian American, Pacific Islander)
    • History of CVD
    • Hypertension (≥140/90 or on therapy)
    • HDL <35 mg/dL and/or triglycerides >250 mg/dL
    • Polycystic ovary syndrome
    • Physical inactivity
    • Conditions associated with insulin resistance (severe obesity, acanthosis nigricans)
    • History of GDM → lifelong screening every 3 years
Prediabetes Management
  • Lifestyle intervention: 7% body weight loss + 150 min/week moderate physical activity (DPP trial: 58% risk reduction vs. 31% with metformin)
  • Metformin consideration: Age 25-59, BMI ≥35, history of GDM, rising HbA1c despite lifestyle changes

topics.title: Pathophysiology
topics.content:
Type 1 DM
  • Autoimmune destruction of pancreatic β-cells mediated by T-lymphocytes (primarily CD8+ cytotoxic T cells)
  • Genetic susceptibility: HLA-DR3, HLA-DR4 (strongest association: HLA-DQ8 and HLA-DQ2)
  • Environmental triggers: Viral infections (enterovirus, coxsackie B), early cow's milk exposure (controversial), vitamin D deficiency
  • Autoantibodies: GAD65 (most sensitive in adults), IA-2, ZnT8, IAA (most common in children <5 years)
  • β-cell destruction is progressive; clinical diabetes manifests when ~80-90% of β-cell mass is lost
Type 2 DM — "Ominous Octet" (DeFronzo)
  1. Decreased insulin secretion (β-cell failure — progressive)
  2. Increased hepatic glucose production (↑ gluconeogenesis)
  3. Decreased peripheral glucose uptake (muscle insulin resistance)
  4. Increased lipolysis (adipocyte insulin resistance → ↑ FFA)
  5. Decreased incretin effect (↓ GLP-1 response)
  6. Increased glucagon secretion (α-cell dysregulation)
  7. Increased renal glucose reabsorption (↑ SGLT2 expression)
  8. Neurotransmitter dysfunction (brain insulin resistance, ↓ dopamine)
Metabolic Syndrome

Central obesity + ≥2 of:

  • Triglycerides ≥150 mg/dL
  • HDL <40 (M) / <50 (F) mg/dL
  • BP ≥130/85 mmHg
  • FPG ≥100 mg/dL

topics.title: Management of Type 2 DM
topics.content:
Glycemic Targets
ParameterTarget (Most Adults)Less Stringent
HbA1c<7%<8% (elderly, comorbid, hypoglycemia-prone, limited life expectancy)
Preprandial glucose80–130 mg/dL—
Peak postprandial glucose<180 mg/dL—

Individualization: More stringent (<6.5%) may be appropriate for newly diagnosed, long life expectancy, no significant CVD. Less stringent (<8%) for elderly, hypoglycemia unawareness, extensive comorbidities.

Pharmacotherapy
First-Line: Metformin
FeatureDetail
Mechanism↓ Hepatic glucose production (activates AMPK), ↑ peripheral insulin sensitivity
DoseStart 500mg daily/BID → titrate to 2000mg/day (max 2550mg)
BenefitsWeight neutral/slight loss, no hypoglycemia, ↓ cardiovascular events (UKPDS), low cost
ContraindicationseGFR <30 (contraindicated); use with caution eGFR 30-45; hold before iodinated contrast
Side effectsGI (nausea, diarrhea — use XR formulation), lactic acidosis (rare), vitamin B12 deficiency (monitor periodically)
Sulfonylureas
FeatureDetail
MechanismBind SUR1 on β-cell → close K-ATP channels → ↑ insulin secretion
ExamplesGlimepiride (1–8mg), Glipizide (2.5–40mg), Glyburide (1.25–20mg)
BenefitsEffective HbA1c reduction (~1-1.5%), low cost
RisksHypoglycemia (especially glyburide — avoid in elderly/CKD), weight gain (2-5 kg)
SGLT2 Inhibitors
FeatureDetail
MechanismBlock SGLT2 in proximal tubule → ↑ glycosuria (reduce renal glucose reabsorption)
ExamplesEmpagliflozin (10-25mg), Dapagliflozin (5-10mg), Canagliflozin (100-300mg)
BenefitsCV mortality reduction (EMPA-REG: empagliflozin), HF hospitalization reduction, renal protection (CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD), weight loss (~2-3 kg), BP reduction
RisksGenital mycotic infections, UTI, volume depletion, euglycemic DKA (rare), Fournier gangrene (rare), lower limb amputation (canagliflozin — CANVAS)
ContraindicationseGFR <20 for glycemic use (but renal/CV benefits persist at lower eGFR)
GLP-1 Receptor Agonists
FeatureDetail
MechanismActivate GLP-1 receptors → ↑ glucose-dependent insulin secretion, ↓ glucagon, ↓ gastric emptying, ↑ satiety
ExamplesLiraglutide (1.2-1.8mg daily), Semaglutide (0.25→0.5→1mg weekly SC; or oral 7→14mg daily), Dulaglutide (0.75-4.5mg weekly), Exenatide (2mg weekly)
BenefitsCV event reduction (LEADER: liraglutide; SUSTAIN-6/SELECT: semaglutide), significant weight loss (5-15%), HbA1c reduction (~1-1.8%)
RisksGI (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea — dose-dependent, usually transient), pancreatitis (rare), medullary thyroid carcinoma (preclinical — contraindicated in MEN2/personal/family hx MTC)
DPP-4 Inhibitors
FeatureDetail
MechanismInhibit DPP-4 enzyme → prolong endogenous GLP-1 and GIP activity
ExamplesSitagliptin (100mg), Saxagliptin (5mg), Linagliptin (5mg), Alogliptin (25mg)
BenefitsWeight neutral, well-tolerated, no hypoglycemia, oral
RisksNasopharyngitis, heart failure (saxagliptin — SAVOR-TIMI 53), pancreatitis (rare), joint pain
NoteModest HbA1c reduction (~0.5-0.8%); linagliptin does NOT require renal dose adjustment
Thiazolidinediones (TZDs)
FeatureDetail
MechanismPPARγ agonists → ↑ insulin sensitivity in adipose, muscle, liver
ExamplesPioglitazone (15-45mg)
BenefitsDurable glycemic control, ↑ HDL, ↓ TG (pioglitazone), potential NASH benefit
RisksWeight gain (2-5 kg), fluid retention/edema, HF exacerbation (contraindicated NYHA III-IV), fracture risk (especially women), bladder cancer (pioglitazone — debated)
Insulin Preparations (Harrison's Table 404-4)
TypeExamplesOnsetPeakDuration
Rapid-actingLispro, Aspart, Glulisine5-15 min1-2 h4-6 h
Ultra-rapidFaster aspart (Fiasp), Lispro-aabc (Lyumjev)2.5 min—5-7 h
Short-actingRegular (Humulin R, Novolin R)30-60 min2-3 h6-8 h
IntermediateNPH2-4 h4-10 h10-16 h
Long-actingGlargine U-100 (Lantus), Detemir (Levemir)2-4 hMinimal20-24 h
Ultra-longGlargine U-300 (Toujeo), Degludec (Tresiba)1-4 hNone>42 h (degludec)
Premixed70/30 (NPH/Regular), 75/25 (Lispro mix)30 minDual10-16 h
Treatment Algorithm (Simplified)
  1. At diagnosis: Lifestyle + Metformin (unless contraindicated)
  2. If HbA1c not at goal after 3 months: Add second agent based on patient profile:
    • ASCVD/high CV risk → SGLT2i or GLP-1 RA (with proven CV benefit)
    • Heart failure → SGLT2i (empagliflozin or dapagliflozin)
    • CKD → SGLT2i (preferred) or GLP-1 RA
    • Weight management priority → GLP-1 RA or dual GIP/GLP-1 RA (tirzepatide)
    • Cost is major issue → Sulfonylurea or TZD
    • Minimizing hypoglycemia → DPP-4i, GLP-1 RA, SGLT2i, or TZD
  3. If still not at goal: Triple therapy or add basal insulin
  4. If basal insulin insufficient: Add prandial insulin (basal-bolus) or GLP-1 RA (basal insulin + GLP-1 RA combination: iDegLira, iGlarLixi)

topics.title: Management of Type 1 DM
topics.content:
Insulin Regimens
  • Basal-bolus (MDI): Basal (glargine/detemir/degludec) + rapid-acting before meals
    • Typical starting dose: 0.4-0.5 units/kg/day total; split ~50% basal / 50% bolus
  • Continuous Subcutaneous Insulin Infusion (CSII): Insulin pump — rapid-acting only
    • Benefits: ↓ HbA1c, ↓ hypoglycemia, ↓ glucose variability, flexible lifestyle
  • Automated Insulin Delivery (AID) / Hybrid Closed-Loop: Pump + CGM + algorithm
    • Adjusts basal rate automatically based on CGM readings
    • User still boluses for meals
Continuous Glucose Monitoring (CGM)
  • Time in Range (TIR) target: >70% time in 70-180 mg/dL
  • Time below range: <4% time <70 mg/dL; <1% time <54 mg/dL
  • GMI (Glucose Management Indicator): CGM-derived estimated A1c
Adjunctive Therapies in T1DM
  • Pramlintide (amylin analog): ↓ postprandial glucose, ↓ glucagon, ↓ gastric emptying, modest weight loss; risk of hypoglycemia
  • SGLT2 inhibitors: NOT FDA-approved for T1DM due to euglycemic DKA risk; used off-label in some countries with caution

topics.title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis
topics.content:
Definition and Diagnostic Criteria
ParameterDKAHHS
Plasma glucose>250 mg/dL>600 mg/dL
Arterial pH<7.3>7.3
Serum bicarbonate<18 mEq/L>18 mEq/L
Urine/serum ketonesPositiveSmall/negative
Serum osmolalityVariable>320 mOsm/kg
Anion gap>12Variable
Mental statusAlert → obtundedStupor → coma
DKA Severity Classification
MildModerateSevere
pH7.25–7.307.00–7.24<7.00
HCO₃15–1810–14<10
Mental statusAlertAlert/drowsyStupor/coma
DKA Precipitating Factors ("5 I's")
  1. Infection (most common — pneumonia, UTI, sepsis)
  2. Insulin omission/non-compliance
  3. Infarction (MI, stroke, mesenteric ischemia)
  4. Intoxication (alcohol, cocaine)
  5. Iatrogenic (steroids, SGLT2 inhibitors, atypical antipsychotics)
DKA Management Protocol
1. Fluid Resuscitation (First Priority)
  • 0.9% NaCl: 1-1.5 L in first hour
  • Then: Corrected Na⁺ based:
    • If corrected Na⁺ low → continue 0.9% NaCl (250-500 mL/h)
    • If corrected Na⁺ normal/high → switch to 0.45% NaCl (250-500 mL/h)
  • When glucose reaches 200 mg/dL: Add D5W (dextrose 5%) to IV fluids
  • Typical deficit: 3-6 L in DKA, 8-10 L in HHS
2. Insulin Therapy
  • Regular insulin IV infusion: 0.1-0.14 units/kg/hr (no bolus needed if starting at 0.14)
  • Alternative: 0.1 units/kg IV bolus → then 0.1 units/kg/hr
  • Target glucose decline: 50-70 mg/dL per hour
  • If glucose not falling: Double infusion rate
  • When glucose 200 mg/dL (DKA) or 300 mg/dL (HHS): Reduce infusion to 0.02-0.05 units/kg/hr; target glucose 150-200 mg/dL until resolution
  • Transition to SC insulin: Overlap IV insulin with SC insulin by 1-2 hours; ensure patient eating
3. Potassium Replacement
  • If K⁺ <3.3 mEq/L: HOLD insulin; replace K⁺ first (20-40 mEq/h) until K⁺ >3.3
  • If K⁺ 3.3-5.3 mEq/L: Give 20-30 mEq K⁺ in each liter of IV fluid (target K⁺ 4-5)
  • If K⁺ >5.3 mEq/L: Do not give K⁺; recheck every 2 hours
4. Bicarbonate
  • Only if pH <6.9: 100 mmol NaHCO₃ in 400 mL H₂O + 20 mEq KCl, infuse over 2 hours
  • pH 6.9-7.0: Consider 50 mmol NaHCO₃
  • pH >7.0: No bicarbonate needed
DKA Resolution Criteria
  • Glucose <200 mg/dL PLUS two of:
    • pH >7.3
    • Bicarbonate ≥15 mEq/L
    • Anion gap ≤12

topics.title: Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State
topics.content:
• More common in T2DM (often elderly, nursing home patients)
  • Mortality higher than DKA (5-20% vs. 1-5%)
  • Pathophysiology: Sufficient insulin to prevent ketosis but insufficient to prevent hyperglycemia → profound osmotic diuresis → severe dehydration
  • Key features: Extreme hyperglycemia (often >600-1000 mg/dL), serum osmolality >320, severe dehydration, altered mental status, minimal/no ketosis
  • Treatment: Similar to DKA but emphasis on aggressive fluid resuscitation (may need 9-10 L); lower insulin doses often sufficient; watch for cerebral edema with rapid correction

topics.title: Hypoglycemia in Diabetes
topics.content:
Classification
LevelDefinition
Level 1Glucose 54-69 mg/dL (alert value)
Level 2Glucose <54 mg/dL (clinically significant)
Level 3Severe event — altered mental/physical status requiring assistance (no specific glucose threshold)
Hypoglycemia Unawareness
  • Occurs after recurrent hypoglycemia → blunted counterregulatory response (HAAF — Hypoglycemia-Associated Autonomic Failure)
  • Treatment: 2-3 weeks of strict hypoglycemia avoidance → partial restoration of awareness
  • Glucagon: IM/SC 1mg or nasal glucagon 3mg for severe episodes; also available as dasiglucagon (SC)

topics.title: Chronic Complications of Diabetes
topics.content:
Microvascular Complications
Diabetic Retinopathy
StageFindings
Mild NPDRMicroaneurysms only
Moderate NPDRMicroaneurysms + hemorrhages + hard exudates + cotton-wool spots
Severe NPDR"4-2-1 rule": hemorrhages in 4 quadrants, venous beading in 2 quadrants, IRMA in 1 quadrant
PDRNeovascularization (disc or elsewhere), vitreous/preretinal hemorrhage
DMEMacular edema — can occur at any stage; leading cause of vision loss
  • Screening: Dilated eye exam at diagnosis (T2DM) or within 5 years of diagnosis (T1DM); then annually
  • Treatment: Anti-VEGF injections (ranibizumab, aflibercept, bevacizumab) for DME and PDR; pan-retinal photocoagulation for PDR
Diabetic Nephropathy (Diabetic Kidney Disease)
  • Earliest sign: Moderately increased albuminuria (UACR 30-300 mg/g — formerly "microalbuminuria")
  • Progression: Normoalbuminuria → moderately increased albuminuria → severely increased albuminuria (>300 mg/g) → declining eGFR → ESKD
  • Screening: Annual UACR + eGFR starting at diagnosis (T2DM) or 5 years after diagnosis (T1DM)
  • Treatment:
    • ACEi or ARB: First-line for any albuminuria (even with normal BP)
    • SGLT2 inhibitors: Recommended when eGFR ≥20 + albuminuria (CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD)
    • Finerenone (non-steroidal MRA): Added benefit on top of RAS blockade (FIDELIO-DKD, FIGARO-DKD)
    • BP target: <130/80 mmHg
    • Avoid: NSAIDs, nephrotoxins; adjust metformin/other drugs for eGFR
Diabetic Neuropathy
TypeFeatures
Distal symmetric polyneuropathy (DSPN)Most common; "stocking-glove" distribution; burning/tingling/numbness; loss of ankle reflexes, vibration, monofilament sensation
Diabetic autonomic neuropathyCardiovascular (resting tachycardia, orthostatic hypotension), GI (gastroparesis, constipation/diarrhea), GU (erectile dysfunction, neurogenic bladder), sudomotor (anhidrosis feet, gustatory sweating)
Focal neuropathiesCranial nerve palsies (CN III most common — pupil-sparing), mononeuropathies (median, ulnar, peroneal), diabetic amyotrophy (lumbosacral radiculoplexus neuropathy)
  • Screening: Annual foot exam with 10g monofilament + one of: vibration (128Hz tuning fork), pinprick, ankle reflexes, vibration perception threshold
  • Treatment of painful neuropathy: Pregabalin, duloxetine, gabapentin (first-line); TCAs (amitriptyline), capsaicin cream, tapentadol
Macrovascular Complications
  • CVD is the leading cause of death in diabetes (2-4x increased risk)
  • Comprehensive CV risk management:
    • Statin therapy: Moderate-intensity for all diabetics 40-75 years; high-intensity if ASCVD present
    • Aspirin: Secondary prevention (established ASCVD); primary prevention only if high CV risk + low bleeding risk
    • BP target: <130/80 mmHg (ACEi/ARB preferred if albuminuria present)
    • Smoking cessation: Essential
Landmark Trials Summary
TrialKey Finding
DCCT (T1DM)Intensive glycemic control (HbA1c ~7%) ↓ microvascular complications by 50-76%
EDIC (DCCT follow-up)"Metabolic memory" — early intensive control provides lasting benefit even after HbA1c equalization
UKPDS (T2DM)Intensive control ↓ microvascular complications; metformin ↓ MI in overweight patients; "legacy effect" on long-term follow-up
ACCORDTargeting HbA1c <6% in high-risk T2DM → increased mortality (intensive arm stopped early)
ADVANCETargeting HbA1c <6.5% → ↓ nephropathy, no excess mortality (used gliclazide MR-based regimen)
VADTIntensive control in veterans with long-standing T2DM → no CV benefit initially; CV benefit emerged after 10-year follow-up
EMPA-REG OUTCOMEEmpagliflozin ↓ CV death by 38%, ↓ HF hospitalization by 35% in T2DM with ASCVD
LEADERLiraglutide ↓ CV death by 22%, ↓ MACE by 13% in T2DM with high CV risk
SUSTAIN-6Semaglutide ↓ MACE by 26% in T2DM with high CV risk
CREDENCECanagliflozin ↓ renal composite endpoint by 30% in T2DM with DKD
DAPA-CKDDapagliflozin ↓ renal composite by 39% — benefit extended to non-diabetic CKD
NICE-SUGARIn critically ill patients, targeting glucose 81-108 mg/dL → increased mortality vs. 140-180 mg/dL

topics.title: Hospital Management of Diabetes
topics.content:
Glycemic Targets in Hospital
SettingTarget
ICU140–180 mg/dL
Non-ICU100–180 mg/dL
Pre-meal (non-ICU)<140 mg/dL
  • More stringent targets (110-140 mg/dL) may be appropriate in select patients (cardiac surgery) IF achievable without hypoglycemia
Insulin in Hospital
  • ICU: Continuous IV insulin infusion (regular insulin) with validated protocol
  • Non-ICU: Basal-bolus-correction regimen preferred over sliding scale alone
    • Typical starting: 0.4-0.5 units/kg/day (reduce to 0.2-0.3 if elderly, CKD, malnourished)
    • 50% basal, 50% divided prandial with correction factor
  • Avoid: Sole use of sliding scale insulin (reactive, not proactive; leads to glycemic variability)
  • Oral agents: Generally held in hospital (exception: stable patients on non-ICU floors in some situations)
Perioperative Management
  • Day of surgery: Hold oral hypoglycemics; give 50-80% of basal insulin dose; hold prandial insulin
  • Metformin: Hold on day of surgery and 48 hours post-contrast
  • SGLT2 inhibitors: Hold 3-4 days before elective surgery (euglycemic DKA risk)
Glucocorticoid-Induced Hyperglycemia
  • Common pattern: Afternoon/evening hyperglycemia (with AM prednisolone dosing)
  • Management: NPH insulin (given with AM steroid — peak matches steroid effect) or increase existing basal insulin; may need prandial coverage
  • Typical starting dose: NPH 0.1-0.2 units/kg per 10mg prednisolone equivalent
Transition from IV to SC Insulin
  • Give first SC insulin dose 1-2 hours BEFORE stopping IV infusion (prevents hyperglycemia gap)
  • Calculate 24h IV insulin use → give 60-80% as total daily SC dose (to account for improved oral intake lag)

topics.title: Monogenic Diabetes
topics.content:
MODY (Maturity-Onset Diabetes of the Young) — ADA Table 2.7
TypeGeneFeaturesTreatment
MODY 1HNF4AProgressive β-cell dysfunction; neonatal macrosomia + transient neonatal hypoglycemiaSulfonylureas (sensitive)
MODY 2GCKMild, stable fasting hyperglycemia (100-145 mg/dL); rarely needs treatmentUsually none (except pregnancy)
MODY 3HNF1AMost common MODY; progressive; low renal threshold for glucose (glycosuria at normal glucose)Sulfonylureas (very sensitive — start low dose)
MODY 5HNF1BRenal cysts/anomalies + diabetes; pancreatic atrophy; genital tract anomaliesInsulin (often required)

When to suspect MODY: Diabetes <25 years, autoantibody-negative, strong family history (autosomal dominant), detectable C-peptide after 3-5 years, no features of T2DM

Neonatal Diabetes (<6 months of age)
GeneFeaturesTreatment
KCNJ11Kir6.2 mutations; most common permanent neonatal DMHigh-dose sulfonylureas (can switch from insulin!)
ABCC8SUR1 mutationsSulfonylureas
INSInsulin gene mutationsInsulin

topics.title: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus
topics.content:
Screening

Two approaches:

StrategyMethodDiagnostic Thresholds
One-step (IADPSG)75g OGTT at 24-28 weeksFasting ≥92, 1h ≥180, 2h ≥153 mg/dL — ANY one value diagnostic
Two-stepStep 1: 50g GCT (non-fasting); if ≥130-140 → Step 2: 100g OGTTCarpenter-Coustan: Fasting ≥95, 1h ≥180, 2h ≥155, 3h ≥140 — TWO or more values diagnostic
Management
  • MNT + exercise first
  • Insulin if targets not met (preferred pharmacotherapy in pregnancy)
    • Targets: Fasting <95 mg/dL; 1h postprandial <140; 2h postprandial <120
  • Metformin/glyburide: Used in some settings but cross placenta; insulin preferred
  • Postpartum: Test for persistent diabetes at 4-12 weeks (75g OGTT); then screen every 1-3 years lifelong

topics.title: Post-Transplant Diabetes Mellitus
topics.content:
• Risk factors: Tacrolimus > cyclosporine, corticosteroids, obesity, HCV, older age, ethnicity
  • Screen: FPG or OGTT (HbA1c unreliable early post-transplant due to blood loss/anemia)
  • Treatment: Lifestyle modification; insulin preferred initially; can transition to oral agents when stable

topics.title: Diabetes and Comorbidities
topics.content:
Comprehensive Medical Evaluation

A complete medical evaluation should be performed at initial visit and follow-up to: confirm diagnosis and classify diabetes, assess glycemic status, evaluate complications and comorbidities, assess social determinants of health, and formulate a care management plan.

Follow-up intervals: Every 3-6 months initially, then at least annually.

Autoimmune Disease Screening in T1DM
  • Thyroid disease: Screen with TSH soon after T1DM diagnosis; repeat at intervals if clinically indicated (high prevalence of autoimmune thyroiditis)
  • Celiac disease: Screen in the presence of GI symptoms, signs, or laboratory findings suggestive (e.g., diarrhea, malabsorption, iron deficiency anemia, osteoporosis, vitamin deficiencies)
  • Other associations: Pernicious anemia (B12 deficiency), Addison disease, vitiligo, autoimmune hepatitis, myasthenia gravis
  • May occur in context of polyglandular autoimmune syndromes
Immunizations in Diabetes
VaccineRecommendation
COVID-19Updated vaccines as recommended for all ≥6 months
Hepatitis BAdults with diabetes aged <60 (recommended); ≥60 (may consider)
InfluenzaAnnual trivalent inactivated vaccine (avoid live attenuated in diabetes)
PneumococcalPPSV23 ages 19-64; PCV15 or PCV20 per CDC schedule; ≥65 if not prior
Zoster (Shingrix)Two-dose series, ≥50 years
RSVSingle dose for adults ≥75, or ≥60 at high risk
TdapBooster every 10 years; extra dose in pregnancy
Bone Health in Diabetes

Fracture risk is increased in both T1DM and T2DM:

FeatureT1DMT2DM
BMDOften LOWNormal or HIGH (paradoxically)
Hip fracture risk4.35× increased1.79× increased
BMD utilityUnderestimates fracture riskUnderestimates fracture risk (T-score ≤-2.0 interpreted as equivalent to ≤-2.5 in non-diabetics)
MechanismLow bone mass, autoimmune factorsImpaired bone microarchitecture, accelerated bone loss despite normal BMD

Screening (ADA Table 4.4): DXA recommended for:

  • All adults ≥65 years
  • Postmenopausal women and men ≥50 with diabetes-specific risk factors: frequent hypoglycemia, diabetes duration >10 years, medications (insulin, TZDs, sulfonylureas), A1C >8%, neuropathy/retinopathy/nephropathy, frequent falls, glucocorticoid use

Management considerations:

  • TZDs increase fracture risk — avoid in women at risk
  • Insulin and sulfonylureas increase fracture risk via hypoglycemia-related falls
  • Bisphosphonates, denosumab, and osteoanabolic agents have similar efficacy as in non-diabetics
  • Denosumab preferred if eGFR <30-35 (bisphosphonates contraindicated)
  • Calcium intake 1,000-1,200 mg/day + vitamin D 600-800 IU/day
MASLD/MASH (formerly NAFLD/NASH)

Prevalence: >70% of T2DM patients have MASLD; 12-20% have "at-risk" MASH (≥F2 fibrosis)

Definition: MASLD = ≥5% hepatic steatosis + ≥1 cardiometabolic risk factor, without excessive alcohol consumption (>21 standard drinks/week men, >14 women)

Fibrosis staging: F0 (no fibrosis), F1 (mild), F2 (moderate/significant), F3 (severe/advanced), F4 (cirrhosis)

Screening Algorithm (ADA Figure 4.2):

  1. Calculate FIB-4 index (age, ALT, AST, platelets) — most cost-effective initial screening
    • FIB-4 <1.3 → Low risk → Repeat every 1-2 years
    • FIB-4 ≥1.3 → Needs further evaluation with liver stiffness measurement (LSM) by transient elastography
    • FIB-4 >2.67 → High risk → Refer to hepatologist directly
  2. If LSM <8.0 kPa → Low risk for advanced fibrosis
  3. If LSM ≥8.0 kPa → High risk (F3-F4) → Refer to liver specialist

Alternative to LSM: Enhanced Liver Fibrosis (ELF) test — ELF <9.8 = low risk; ELF ≥9.8 = high risk

Management (ADA Figure 4.3 — MASLD Treatment Algorithm):

MASLD StageObesity RxDiabetes RxMASH Rx
F0-F1GLP-1 RA, dual GIP/GLP-1 RAGLP-1 RA, dual GIP/GLP-1 RA, pioglitazone, SGLT2iNot indicated
F2-F3 ("at-risk")GLP-1 RA, dual GIP/GLP-1 RAGLP-1 RA, dual GIP/GLP-1 RA, pioglitazoneResmetirom
Compensated cirrhosisAs F2-F3 with cautionAs F2-F3 with cautionAVOID
Decompensated cirrhosisAVOIDOnly insulinAVOID

Resmetirom: Thyroid hormone receptor-β agonist, FDA-approved for MASLD with moderate (F2) or advanced (F3) liver fibrosis (not cirrhosis). Phase 3 RCT: resolution of MASH without worsening fibrosis in 29.9% (100mg) vs 9.7% placebo. May decrease free T4 and increase SHBG.

Key pharmacotherapy points:
  • Pioglitazone: Improves MASH histology; reverses steatohepatitis; but weight gain, fracture risk, HF risk
  • GLP-1 RAs: Semaglutide resolved steatohepatitis in 59% (2.4 mg/week) vs 17% placebo; slowed fibrosis progression
  • SGLT2 inhibitors: Reduce hepatic steatosis but effect on steatohepatitis unknown
  • Metformin: No benefit for MASH
  • Vitamin E: May help MASH in non-diabetics; ineffective in T2DM
  • Weight loss ≥5% improves steatosis; ≥10% may improve fibrosis
  • Statin therapy is safe in compensated cirrhosis; use with caution in decompensated
Cognitive Impairment and Dementia
  • Diabetes increases dementia risk: 43% higher all-type dementia, 43% higher Alzheimer, 91% higher vascular dementia
  • "Diabetes-related dementia" — distinct entity with slow progression, absence of typical AD neuroimaging, lean body habitus, high A1C, long duration, insulin use
  • Higher A1C → lower cognitive function; intensive glycemic management shows modest cognitive benefit
  • Severe hypoglycemia increases risk of long-term psychomotor and cognitive decline
  • In cognitive impairment: simplify regimens, liberalize A1C goals, minimize hypoglycemia risk
Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA)
  • Prevalence in T2DM: up to 23%; with obesity may reach 58%
  • OSA is an independent risk factor for CVD
  • Screen with suggestive symptoms (excessive daytime sleepiness, snoring, witnessed apnea)
  • Treatment (CPAP, lifestyle, oral appliances) improves quality of life and BP
  • Tirzepatide (dual GIP/GLP-1 RA) reduced sleep apnea severity in two phase 3 RCTs
Sexual Dysfunction in Diabetes

Erectile dysfunction (ED) in men:

  • Prevalence: 52.5% in men with diabetes; best predictors: age >40, CVD, hypertension, obesity, metabolic syndrome
  • Screen men with diabetes for ED, particularly with high CV risk, neuropathy, retinopathy
  • Check morning total testosterone if symptoms of hypogonadism
  • First-line treatment: PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil); may be less responsive in diabetes
  • Low testosterone: replacement may improve sexual function, well-being, muscle mass, bone density

Female sexual dysfunction (FSD) in women:

  • More common in diabetes (OR 2.27 T1DM; 2.49 T2DM vs. non-diabetic)
  • Screen postmenopausal women for menopausal symptoms including vaginal dryness and dyspareunia
  • Management: lifestyle interventions, hormonal therapies, addressing glycemic control and psychosocial factors
Cancer Risk
  • Diabetes associated with increased risk of cancers of: liver, pancreas, endometrium, colon/rectum, breast, bladder
  • Metformin may have anticancer properties
  • GLP-1 RAs: No association with thyroid cancer, pancreatic cancer, or any other cancer in humans
  • Encourage recommended age/sex-appropriate cancer screenings

topics.title: Diabetic Kidney Disease — Expanded Management
topics.content:
CKD Staging in Diabetes

CKD is classified by GFR (G stage) and Albuminuria (A stage):

GFR CategoryeGFR (mL/min/1.73m²)Description
G1≥90Normal or high
G260-89Mildly decreased
G3a45-59Mildly to moderately decreased
G3b30-44Moderately to severely decreased
G415-29Severely decreased
G5<15Kidney failure
Albuminuria CategoryUACR (mg/g)Description
A1<30Normal to mildly increased
A230-299Moderately increased
A3≥300Severely increased
When to Suspect Non-Diabetic Kidney Disease
  • T1DM duration <5 years
  • Active urine sediment (red/white cells, cellular casts)
  • Chronically well-managed blood glucose
  • Rapidly declining eGFR
  • Rapidly increasing or very high UACR or urine protein/creatinine
  • No retinopathy in T1DM patient

→ Refer to nephrologist for further workup including possible kidney biopsy

Screening for CKD Complications
ComplicationEvaluation
BP >130/80 mmHgBP, weight, BMI
Volume overloadHistory, physical examination, weight
Electrolyte abnormalitiesSerum electrolytes
Metabolic acidosisSerum electrolytes
AnemiaHemoglobin, iron studies, ferritin
Metabolic bone diseaseCalcium, phosphate, PTH, vitamin D
  • Complications become prevalent below eGFR 60 (G3 or greater)
  • Monitoring frequency: G3 CKD every 6-12 months; G4 every 3-5 months; G5 every 1-3 months
Interventions That Lower Albuminuria
  • Blood glucose management
  • Blood pressure management
  • ACE inhibitors or ARBs
  • Smoking cessation
  • Weight loss
  • Changes in eating patterns (decreased salt and/or protein intake)
  • SGLT2 inhibitors, MRAs, or GLP-1 RAs
Pharmacotherapy for DKD
ACE Inhibitors / ARBs
  • First-line for BP control + renoprotection when albuminuria present
  • Recommended for: hypertension + eGFR <60 + UACR ≥300 (strongly) or UACR 30-299 (recommended)
  • Titrate to maximum tolerated doses
  • Continue even with mild-moderate serum creatinine rise (≤30%) in absence of volume depletion
  • Do NOT combine ACEi + ARB (no benefit, higher adverse events — ONTARGET)
  • NOT recommended for primary prevention in absence of hypertension or albuminuria
  • Monitor potassium and creatinine 7-14 days after initiation or dose change
SGLT2 Inhibitors for CKD
  • Recommended for T2DM + CKD with eGFR ≥20 mL/min/1.73m²
  • Continue until dialysis or transplant (new 2025 recommendation)
  • Key trials:
    • CREDENCE (canagliflozin): 32% reduction in ESKD composite in T2DM + UACR ≥300 + eGFR 30-90
    • DAPA-CKD (dapagliflozin): 39% reduction in kidney composite; benefit extends to non-diabetic CKD
    • EMPA-KIDNEY (empagliflozin): Benefit in patients with eGFR as low as 20; cardiovascular deaths reduced; HR 0.72
  • Renal and CV benefits persist at eGFR levels where glucose-lowering effect is minimal
  • Initial "dip" in eGFR is expected (hemodynamic, not structural) — do not discontinue
Finerenone (Non-steroidal MRA)
  • Recommended for T2DM + CKD + albuminuria already on ACEi/ARB at max tolerated dose
  • eGFR ≥25 mL/min/1.73m² and serum potassium ≤4.8 mmol/L
  • FIDELIO-DKD: Significant reduction in CKD progression AND cardiovascular events; primary endpoint: 18% reduction in kidney composite (sustained ≥40% eGFR decrease, kidney failure, renal death)
  • FIGARO-DKD: 13% reduction in primary CV composite (driven by 29% reduction in HF hospitalizations); 23% reduction in kidney composite
  • Pooled FIDELITY analysis (N=13,171): 14% reduction in CV composite; 23% reduction in kidney composite
  • Hyperkalemia: 10.8% vs 5.3% (finerenone vs placebo); discontinuation only 1.2%
  • Can be used interchangeably with or in addition to SGLT2 inhibitors
  • No direct comparison studies of MRAs vs SGLT2 inhibitors vs GLP-1 RAs
GLP-1 Receptor Agonists for CKD
  • Reduce CV risk and may slow CKD progression
  • FLOW trial (semaglutide): First GLP-1 RA trial with primary kidney endpoint
    • 3,533 patients with significant CKD (eGFR 25-75 + albuminuria ≥100 mg/g)
    • 24% lower HR for primary kidney composite (≥50% eGFR decline, ESKD, renal death, cardiovascular death)
    • Stopped early for efficacy
    • CV deaths comprised ~38% of events
  • Suggested for CV risk reduction when such risk is predominant concern
  • May need dose adjustment at low eGFR
Metformin in CKD (FDA Guidance)
  1. Contraindicated if eGFR <30 mL/min/1.73m²
  2. Monitor eGFR while taking metformin
  3. Reassess benefits/risks when eGFR falls to <45
  4. Do not initiate if eGFR <45
  5. Temporarily discontinue before iodinated contrast procedures with eGFR 30-60
Holistic Approach to Diabetes + CKD

First-line drug therapy (all patients with T2DM + CKD):

  • SGLT2 inhibitor (initiate if eGFR ≥20, continue until dialysis/transplant)
  • Metformin (if eGFR ≥30)
  • RAS inhibitor at maximum tolerated dose (if albuminuria and/or hypertension)
  • Moderate or high-intensity statin

Additional risk-based therapy:

  • GLP-1 RA (if needed for glycemic goal or CV risk reduction)
  • Non-steroidal MRA (finerenone — if ACR ≥30 mg/g and normal potassium)
  • Dihydropyridine CCB and/or diuretic (if needed for BP goal)
  • Antiplatelet agent (if clinical ASCVD)
  • Ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitor, or icosapent ethyl (if indicated for ASCVD risk and lipids)
Referral to Nephrologist

Consider referral when:

  • Continuously rising UACR and/or continuously declining eGFR
  • Uncertainty about etiology of kidney disease
  • Difficult management issues (anemia, secondary hyperparathyroidism, metabolic bone disease, resistant hypertension, electrolyte disturbances)
  • Advanced CKD: eGFR <30 mL/min/1.73m² (stage G4)
  • Need for renal replacement therapy planning

topics.title: Diabetes Technology
topics.content:
Blood Glucose Monitoring (BGM)
  • Indicated for all patients on insulin; less clear benefit for non-insulin-treated T2DM
  • Frequency: T1DM on MDI/pump — 6-10 times daily (or use CGM); T2DM on basal insulin — at least fasting glucose daily
  • Accuracy standards: FDA requires 95% of readings within 15% for BG ≥75 mg/dL and within 15 mg/dL for BG <75 mg/dL
  • Substances causing falsely high readings: maltose, galactose, xylose, N-acetylcysteine, acetaminophen (at low BG), dopamine (at low BG)
  • Substances causing falsely low readings: furosemide, uric acid (at very low/very high BG), high hematocrit
  • Vitamin C: Can cause falsely lower OR higher readings depending on assay
Continuous Glucose Monitoring (CGM)

Types of CGM devices (Table 7.3):

TypeDescription
rtCGM (real-time)Continuous display of glucose; includes alarms. Dexcom G6/G7, Medtronic Guardian
isCGM (intermittently scanned)Requires scanning to view data; FreeStyle Libre 2/3. Newer models have optional alarms
Professional CGMClinic-based; worn 7-14 days for pattern analysis; may be blinded or unblinded
Over-the-counter CGMBiosensors with insights (not alarms); for prediabetes/non-insulin diabetes

CGM recommendations:

  • Offer to all T1DM patients early in disease, even at diagnosis
  • Recommend rtCGM or isCGM for T2DM on insulin therapy
  • Consider for T2DM on non-insulin glucose-lowering medications
  • Use rtCGM as close to daily as possible; scan isCGM at minimum every 8 hours
Key CGM metrics (International Consensus on Time in Range):
  • Time in Range (TIR): >70% between 70-180 mg/dL (correlates with A1C ~7%)
  • Time Below Range (TBR): <4% below 70 mg/dL; <1% below 54 mg/dL
  • Time Above Range (TAR): <25% above 180 mg/dL; <5% above 250 mg/dL
  • Glucose Management Indicator (GMI): CGM-derived estimated A1c
  • Coefficient of Variation (CV): Target ≤36% (glycemic variability)

CGM Interfering Substances (Table 7.4):

SubstanceEffect
Acetaminophen (>4g/day)Higher sensor readings (Dexcom G6/G7, Medtronic Guardian)
Vitamin C (>500 mg/day)Higher sensor readings (FreeStyle Libre 14-day, Libre 2, Libre 3)
Vitamin C (>1000 mg/day)Higher sensor readings (FreeStyle Libre 2 Plus, Libre 3 Plus)
HydroxyureaHigher sensor readings (Dexcom G6/G7, Medtronic Guardian)
Mannitol (IV/peritoneal dialysis)Higher sensor readings (Senseonics Eversense)
Insulin Delivery Systems

Insulin syringes and pens:

  • Pens preferred in most MDI patients for convenience, accuracy, reduced pain
  • Connected insulin pens: Log doses automatically, sync with CGM, provide reports
  • Needle gauge: 22-34 (higher = thinner); length 4-12.7 mm (shorter 4-5 mm needles reduce IM injection risk)

Insulin pumps and Automated Insulin Delivery (AID) systems:

SystemDescription
Standard pump (CSII)Delivers rapid-acting insulin via preset basal rates + manual boluses
Sensor-Augmented Pump (SAP)Pump + CGM with low-glucose suspend (insulin stops when glucose low/predicted low)
Hybrid Closed-Loop (AID)Pump + CGM + algorithm that auto-adjusts basal rates; user still boluses for meals

AID systems (current recommendation):

  • Preferred insulin delivery method for T1DM in youth and adults
  • Available for T2DM (one system FDA-approved)
  • Glucose targets typically 100-120 mg/dL (some adjustable to 150 mg/dL)
  • Consistent improvements: ↓ A1C, ↑ TIR (especially overnight), ↓ hypoglycemia, improved quality of life
  • Greatest improvements seen in those with highest baseline A1C or lowest baseline TIR

Open-source AID: OpenAPS and Loop algorithms

  • Community-developed; provide precise control code
  • Supported by large real-world studies and a multicenter RCT
  • Health care professionals should support patients choosing these systems
Inpatient Use of Diabetes Technology
  • Continue personal CGM during hospitalization with confirmatory point-of-care glucose for insulin dosing
  • Continue insulin pump/AID if patient is alert, capable, and supplies are available
  • Point-of-care BGM remains the approved inpatient standard

topics.title: Bone Structure and Remodeling
topics.content:
Bone is a dynamic tissue remodeled constantly throughout life. It consists of compact (cortical) bone forming the cylindrical shell of long bones, and cancellous (trabecular) bone forming the plate-like meshwork internally.

Extracellular Matrix
  • Organic matrix (~90-95% is type I collagen) + noncollagenous proteins (osteocalcin, osteopontin, fibronectin, thrombospondin)
  • Mineral phase: Poorly crystalline hydroxyapatite [calcium and phosphate]
  • Primary mineralization occurs soon after matrix secretion; secondary mineralization takes weeks to months
Key Cell Types
CellOriginFunction
OsteoblastsMesenchymal stem cellsSynthesize organic matrix, regulate mineralization; express Runx2, secrete collagen I, ALP, osteocalcin
OsteocytesTerminally differentiated osteoblastsMechanosensors; secrete FGF23 (major regulator of phosphate); secrete sclerostin (inhibits Wnt → inhibits bone formation)
OsteoclastsHematopoietic lineage (monocyte/macrophage precursors)Resorb bone in Howship's lacunae; require M-CSF and RANK ligand for differentiation; secrete acid + cathepsin K
RANK/RANKL/OPG System
  • RANK ligand (on osteoblasts/stromal cells) binds RANK (on osteoclast precursors) → stimulates osteoclast differentiation and activation
  • Osteoprotegerin (OPG) = soluble decoy receptor for RANKL → inhibits osteoclast formation
  • PTH and 1,25(OH)₂D increase RANKL expression → ↑ osteoclast activity
Bone Remodeling Cycle
  • Resorption (~3 weeks) → Reversal → Bone Formation (~3 months) → Mineralization
  • Basic multicellular unit (BMU): coordinated group of osteoclasts and osteoblasts
  • In adults, ~4% of trabecular surface and ~18% of total skeletal calcium turned over per year
Bone Markers
MarkerReflects
Bone-specific ALPOsteoblast activity (formation)
OsteocalcinOsteoblast activity (formation)
P1NP (procollagen I N-terminal propeptide)Bone formation
CTX / NTX (cross-linked peptides)Collagen degradation (resorption)
Hydroxyproline (urine)Bone resorption

topics.title: Calcium Metabolism
topics.content:
Calcium Distribution
  • Total body calcium: 1-2 kg, 99% in skeleton
  • Serum total calcium: 2.2-2.6 mM (8.5-10.5 mg/dL)
  • ~50% ionized (biologically active), ~40% protein-bound (mainly albumin), ~10% complexed with anions
  • Correction for albumin: Adjust total calcium by 0.8 mg/dL for each 1 g/dL deficit in serum albumin
Calcium Homeostasis — Three Hormonal Regulators
HormoneSourceAction on Ca²⁺Mechanism
PTHParathyroid glands↑ serum Ca²⁺↑ bone resorption, ↑ renal Ca reabsorption (DCT), ↑ 1,25(OH)₂D synthesis
1,25(OH)₂DKidney (proximal tubule)↑ serum Ca²⁺↑ intestinal Ca absorption (via calbindin, TRPV5/6), ↑ bone resorption
CalcitoninThyroid C cells↓ serum Ca²⁺Directly inhibits osteoclasts (physiologic role in humans is minimal)
Renal Calcium Handling
  • ~8-10 g/d filtered; 2-3% appears in urine
  • Proximal tubule: 65% reabsorbed (passive, paracellular, coupled to NaCl)
  • Thick ascending limb (cTAL): 20% reabsorbed (paracellular, requires paracellin-1; inhibited by CaSR activation)
  • Distal convoluted tubule (DCT): ~10% reabsorbed (transcellular: TRPV5 → calbindin-D28k → Ca²⁺-ATPase/Na⁺-Ca²⁺ exchanger; stimulated by PTH)
Intestinal Calcium Absorption
  • Active transport (duodenum/proximal jejunum): Controlled by 1,25(OH)₂D via TRPV6 channels; accounts for 20-70% of dietary calcium
  • Passive transport (paracellular): Throughout small intestine; nonsaturable, ~5% of daily intake
  • Optimal absorption requires gastric acid (calcium carbonate needs acid; calcium citrate does not)

topics.title: Phosphorus Metabolism
topics.content:
• ~85% of body phosphorus in bone; also major intracellular constituent
  • Serum phosphate normal range: 0.75-1.45 mmol/L (2.5-4.5 mg/dL)
  • Circadian rhythm: nadir between 7-10 AM; measurements should be fasting
Renal Phosphate Handling
  • Filtered load reabsorbed mainly in proximal tubule via NaPi-2a and NaPi-2c co-transporters
  • Fractional excretion normally 10-15%
  • PTH → ↓ NaPi-2a/2c → ↑ phosphate excretion (phosphaturic)
  • FGF23 → ↓ phosphate reabsorption + ↓ 1α-hydroxylase → ↓ 1,25(OH)₂D synthesis
FGF23 — The Phosphatonin
  • Produced primarily by osteocytes
  • High-phosphate diets ↑ FGF23; low-phosphate diets ↓ FGF23
  • Requires co-receptor α-Klotho for signaling
  • Activating FGF23 mutations → autosomal dominant hypophosphatemic rickets (ADHR)
  • XLH (X-linked hypophosphatemia): Most common genetic cause; due to inactivating mutations in PHEX; high FGF23 levels; treated with burosumab (anti-FGF23 monoclonal antibody)
  • TIO (tumor-induced osteomalacia): Mesenchymal tumors secrete FGF23 → hypophosphatemia; resolves with tumor resection

topics.title: Hypophosphatemia
topics.content:
Causes
  • Reduced renal reabsorption: Primary/secondary hyperparathyroidism, FGF23 excess (XLH, ADHR, TIO), Fanconi's syndrome
  • Impaired intestinal absorption: Antacids (aluminum-containing), sevelamer
  • Shifts into cells: IV glucose, insulin therapy, DKA treatment, refeeding syndrome, respiratory alkalosis, catecholamines
Clinical Features
  • Severe (<0.25 mmol/L; <0.8 mg/dL): Rhabdomyolysis, muscle weakness, confusion, seizures, respiratory failure, cardiac dysfunction, hemolysis
  • Chronic: Bone pain, osteomalacia, pseudofractures, proximal myopathy, rickets (children)
Treatment
  • Severe (<0.75 mmol/L): IV phosphate (sodium or potassium phosphate salts; 0.2-0.8 mmol/kg over 6 h); monitor calcium and phosphate every 6-12 h
  • Mild/moderate: Oral phosphate 750-2000 mg/d in divided doses
  • XLH: Burosumab (anti-FGF23 antibody) — corrects hypophosphatemia, improves bone pain, heals fractures

topics.title: Hyperphosphatemia
topics.content:
Causes
  • Impaired renal excretion: Renal insufficiency (most common), hypoparathyroidism, acromegaly, tumoral calcinosis
  • Massive ECF phosphate loads: Rhabdomyolysis, tumor lysis syndrome, fulminant hepatitis, hemolytic anemia
  • Transcellular shifts: Metabolic acidosis, respiratory acidosis
Clinical Features
  • Acute severe: Widespread calcium phosphate precipitation → hypocalcemia → tetany, seizures, nephrocalcinosis, cardiac calcifications, heart block
  • Levels can reach 7 mmol/L (20 mg/dL) in tumor lysis or massive soft tissue injury
Treatment
  • Volume expansion, aluminum hydroxide antacids or sevelamer to bind intestinal phosphate
  • Hemodialysis: Most effective for severe hyperphosphatemia, especially with renal failure

topics.title: Magnesium Metabolism
topics.content:
• Normal serum Mg: 0.7-1.0 mmol/L (1.7-2.4 mg/dL); 30% protein-bound, 15% complexed
  • Total body: ~25 g (1000 mmol); 50% in bone
  • Dietary absorption: 30-40% in jejunum/ileum; stimulated by 1,25(OH)₂D
Hypomagnesemia

Causes (Table 409-4): GI losses (diarrhea, malabsorption, PPI use), renal losses (Gitelman syndrome, Bartter syndrome, loop/thiazide diuretics, cisplatin, aminoglycosides, cyclosporine, cetuximab), alcoholism, diabetes mellitus

Clinical features: Tetany, seizures, muscle weakness, ataxia, nystagmus, cardiac arrhythmias (prolonged PR/QT, T-wave flattening), ↑ digitalis sensitivity

  • Usually symptomatic at <0.5 mmol/L (1.2 mg/dL)
  • Causes functional hypoparathyroidism: Impairs PTH secretion AND causes PTH resistance → hypocalcemia that is refractory until Mg is corrected

Treatment: Oral MgCl₂/MgO for mild cases (20-30 mmol/d); IV MgCl₂ for severe (50 mmol/d; reduce by 50% if GFR reduced)

Hypermagnesemia

Causes (Table 409-5): Renal failure + Mg intake (antacids, cathartics), massive tissue injury, adrenal insufficiency

Clinical features: Vasodilation, hypotension at >2 mmol/L; loss of deep tendon reflexes at >4 mmol/L; respiratory paralysis, heart block at >5 mmol/L; cardiac arrest approaching 10 mmol/L

Treatment: IV calcium (100-200 mg over 1-2 h) for acute symptoms; hemodialysis for severe cases with renal failure

topics.title: Vitamin D
topics.content:
Synthesis and Metabolism
  1. Skin: 7-dehydrocholesterol → Vitamin D₃ (cholecalciferol) via UV-B radiation
  2. Liver: Vitamin D₃ → 25(OH)D (calcidiol) via 25-hydroxylase (CYP2R1); this is the major circulating/storage form; half-life ~2-3 weeks
  3. Kidney: 25(OH)D → 1,25(OH)₂D (calcitriol) via 1α-hydroxylase (CYP27B1); tightly regulated active hormone

Regulation of 1α-hydroxylase:

  • ↑ by: PTH, hypophosphatemia, low calcium
  • ↓ by: FGF23, 1,25(OH)₂D itself (negative feedback), hypercalcemia, hyperphosphatemia

Inactivation: 24-hydroxylase (CYP24A1) converts both 25(OH)D and 1,25(OH)₂D to inactive metabolites

Actions of 1,25(OH)₂D
  • Intestine: ↑ calcium absorption (via calbindin 9K, TRPV5/6) and phosphate absorption
  • Bone: ↑ osteocalcin and osteopontin expression; induces RANKL → ↑ osteoclast activity → bone resorption
  • Parathyroid: Suppresses PTH gene transcription (therapeutic basis for treating secondary hyperparathyroidism)
  • Immune modulation: Antiproliferative effects on keratinocytes, breast cancer cells, prostate cancer cells
Vitamin D Deficiency

Definition: 25(OH)D level:

  • Deficient: <20 ng/mL (<50 nmol/L)
  • Insufficient: 20-30 ng/mL
  • Sufficient: >30 ng/mL (>75 nmol/L) — some recommend >50 nmol/L as sufficient

Causes (Table 409-6): Inadequate sun/dietary intake, malabsorption (celiac, short gut, gastric bypass), accelerated metabolism (barbiturates, phenytoin, rifampin via CYP3A4 induction), impaired 25-hydroxylation (liver disease), impaired 1α-hydroxylation (CKD), nephrotic syndrome (loss of vitamin D-binding protein), obesity

Clinical manifestations:

  • Children: Rickets — growth plate expansion ("rachitic rosary"), widened wrists, bowed legs, delayed fontanelle closure, hypocalcemic seizures
  • Adults: Osteomalacia — bone pain, proximal myopathy, pseudofractures (Looser's zones), decreased BMD
  • Associated with secondary hyperparathyroidism → further bone loss

Treatment:

  • Recommended daily intake: 600 IU (1-70 years); 800 IU (>70 years)
  • Severe deficiency: 50,000 IU weekly for 3-12 weeks → 800 IU/d maintenance
  • If 1α-hydroxylation impaired (CKD): Use calcitriol (0.25-0.5 μg/d) or doxercalciferol (2.5-5 μg/d)
  • Always co-supplement calcium; monitor urinary calcium (target 100-250 mg/24h)
  • Vitamin D toxicity rare below 40,000 IU/d; presents with hypercalcemia

topics.title: PTH — Structure and Physiology
topics.content:
• 84-amino-acid peptide; amino-terminal PTH(1-34) is sufficient for biologic activity
  • Synthesized as preproPTH (115 aa) → proPTH → mature PTH (84 aa)
  • Half-life of intact PTH: ~minutes; cleared rapidly by liver and kidney
  • CaSR (calcium-sensing receptor): GPCR on parathyroid cells; high Ca²⁺ activates CaSR → suppresses PTH secretion; low Ca²⁺ → ↑ PTH
PTH Actions
  • Bone: ↑ osteoclast activity (indirect via RANKL on osteoblasts); continuous PTH → net resorption; intermittent PTH → net bone formation (anabolic — basis for teriparatide therapy)
  • Kidney: ↑ Ca reabsorption (DCT), ↑ phosphate excretion (proximal tubule via ↓ NaPi-2a), ↑ 1α-hydroxylase → ↑ 1,25(OH)₂D
  • Intestine (indirect): Via ↑ 1,25(OH)₂D → ↑ calcium absorption
PTHrP (PTH-related Peptide)
  • Shares amino-terminal homology with PTH; activates same PTH/PTHrP receptor (PTHR1)
  • Normally paracrine factor (growth plate, breast, smooth muscle)
  • Primary mediator of humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy (HHM) — especially squamous cell carcinomas (lung, kidney, head/neck)

topics.title: Hypercalcemia
topics.content:
Classification of Causes
CategoryKey Causes
Parathyroid-relatedPrimary hyperparathyroidism (adenoma ~80%, hyperplasia ~15-20%, carcinoma ~1%), MEN syndromes, lithium, FHH
Malignancy-relatedHHM (PTHrP-secreting tumors), osteolytic metastases (breast), hematologic (myeloma, lymphoma — via 1,25(OH)₂D or local osteolysis)
Vitamin D-relatedVitamin D intoxication, sarcoidosis/granulomatous diseases (↑ 1,25(OH)₂D from macrophages), Williams syndrome
High bone turnoverHyperthyroidism, immobilization, thiazides, vitamin A intoxication
Renal failureSevere secondary/tertiary hyperparathyroidism, aluminum intoxication, milk-alkali syndrome
Clinical Features of Hypercalcemia
  • Mild (<3.0 mmol/L; <12 mg/dL): Often asymptomatic
  • Moderate (3.0-3.5 mmol/L): Fatigue, depression, confusion, anorexia, nausea, constipation, polyuria, short QT
  • Severe (>3.5 mmol/L; >14 mg/dL): Medical emergency — dehydration, obtundation, coma, cardiac arrest, renal failure, calcifications

"Stones, bones, abdominal moans, and psychic groans" — classic mnemonic for hyperparathyroidism

topics.title: Primary Hyperparathyroidism
topics.content:
Epidemiology & Etiology
  • Most common cause of hypercalcemia in outpatients
  • Solitary adenoma: ~80% of cases
  • Multigland hyperplasia: ~15-20% (associated with MEN syndromes)
  • Parathyroid carcinoma: ~1%; suspect if calcium >3.5 mmol/L (14-15 mg/dL)
  • Annual incidence ~0.2% in patients >60 years; F > M
Genetic Associations
  • MEN 1 (MENIN gene, 11q13): Hyperparathyroidism + pituitary tumors + pancreatic tumors (Wermer's syndrome)
  • MEN 2A (RET proto-oncogene): Medullary thyroid carcinoma + pheochromocytoma + hyperparathyroidism
  • HPT-JT (HRPT2/CDC73 gene): Parathyroid tumors + jaw tumors; higher risk of carcinoma
Diagnosis
  • ↑ Serum calcium + ↑ or inappropriately normal PTH (should be suppressed)
  • ↓ Serum phosphate (due to PTH-induced phosphaturia)
  • ↑ 1,25(OH)₂D in many patients
  • ↑ Urinary calcium (but <99% fractional excretion — distinguishes from FHH)
Guidelines for Surgery in Asymptomatic PHPT

Surgery recommended if ANY of:

ParameterThreshold
Serum calcium>1 mg/dL above normal
RenalCrCl <60 mL/min, 24-h urine Ca >400 mg/d, nephrolithiasis/nephrocalcinosis
SkeletalT-score <-2.5 at lumbar spine, total hip, femoral neck, or distal 1/3 radius; vertebral fracture
Age<50 years
Treatment
  • Parathyroidectomy: Definitive; cure rate >95% for experienced surgeons
  • Minimally invasive approach: Preoperative sestamibi + 4D-CT localization; intraoperative PTH monitoring (>50% drop confirms cure)
  • Medical surveillance (if not surgical): Annual serum calcium, creatinine; bone density every 1-2 years
  • Calcimimetics (cinacalcet): Activates CaSR → ↓ PTH; lowers calcium but does NOT improve BMD

topics.title: Familial Hypocalciuric Hypercalcemia
topics.content:
• Autosomal dominant; inactivating mutations in CaSR (FHH1), Gα11 (FHH2), or AP2S1 (FHH3)
  • Mild hypercalcemia, low-normal PTH, urinary calcium excretion <99% fractional excretion (Ca/Cr clearance ratio <0.01)
  • Key distinction from PHPT: FHH has >99% renal calcium reabsorption
  • Benign; parathyroidectomy NOT indicated (except rare homozygous neonatal severe hyperparathyroidism)

topics.title: Malignancy-Related Hypercalcemia
topics.content:
Two Main Mechanisms
  1. Humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy (HHM): PTHrP secretion by solid tumors (squamous cell carcinoma, renal, breast); PTH low/undetectable, PTHrP elevated, 1,25(OH)₂D low/normal
  2. Local osteolytic hypercalcemia: Direct bone destruction by metastases (breast) or marrow-based malignancies (myeloma); cytokines (RANKL, IL-1, TNF) stimulate osteoclasts
Diagnostic Clues
  • Rapid onset (<6 months); weight loss, known malignancy
  • PTH suppressed/undetectable; PTHrP elevated in HHM
  • In lymphoma/granulomatous disease: ↑ 1,25(OH)₂D

topics.title: Treatment of Hypercalcemia
topics.content:
SeverityApproach
Mild (≤3 mmol/L; ≤12 mg/dL)Hydration; avoid thiazides, lithium, immobilization
Moderate (3.0-3.5 mmol/L)IV normal saline (200-300 mL/h) + loop diuretic (after rehydration) + IV bisphosphonate
Severe (≥3.7 mmol/L; ≥15 mg/dL)Emergency: aggressive IV saline + zoledronate/pamidronate + denosumab + calcitonin (for rapid effect in first 24-48h); consider dialysis if renal failure
Key Agents
DrugOnsetDurationNotes
Zoledronate (4 mg IV)1-2 days>3 weeksMore rapid and sustained than pamidronate
Pamidronate (30-90 mg IV)1-2 days10-14 daysFever in 20%; hypophosphatemia
Denosumab (120 mg SC)1-2 days>3 weeksStrongest antiresorptive; useful if bisphosphonate-refractory
Calcitonin (2-8 U/kg SC/IM q6-12h)Hours1-2 daysRapid but transient (tachyphylaxis); useful bridge
GlucocorticoidsDaysDays-weeksEffective for vitamin D-mediated (sarcoidosis), lymphoma, myeloma; NOT effective for PHPT

topics.title: Hypocalcemia
topics.content:
Pathophysiology & Causes
CategoryConditions
PTH AbsentHereditary hypoparathyroidism (DiGeorge, AIRE mutations/APS-1), acquired hypoparathyroidism (post-surgical — most common acquired cause), hypomagnesemia
PTH IneffectiveCKD (↓ 1,25(OH)₂D), vitamin D deficiency, vitamin D-dependent rickets type I/II, pseudohypoparathyroidism (PHP)
PTH OverwhelmedSevere hyperphosphatemia, tumor lysis, rhabdomyolysis, osteitis fibrosa after parathyroidectomy ("hungry bone syndrome")
Clinical Features
  • Neuromuscular: Muscle spasms, carpopedal spasm, facial grimacing, laryngospasm, seizures
  • Chvostek's sign: Twitching of facial muscles on tapping facial nerve
  • Trousseau's sign: Carpal spasm with BP cuff inflation (more specific)
  • Cardiac: Prolonged QT interval, arrhythmias, reduced cardiac contractility
  • Chronic: Basal ganglia calcification, cataracts, alopecia/candidiasis (in APS-1), dental enamel defects
Treatment

Acute symptomatic hypocalcemia:

  • IV calcium gluconate: 1-2 g (90-180 mg elemental Ca) over 10-20 min, then continuous infusion 0.5-2 mg/kg/h
  • Always correct hypomagnesemia (hypocalcemia is refractory until Mg replete)
  • Monitor for hyperkalemia and digoxin toxicity

Chronic hypoparathyroidism:

  • Oral calcium (1.5-3 g/d elemental) + calcitriol (0.25-1 μg/d) or vitamin D (high dose)
  • Target: Low-normal serum calcium to avoid hypercalciuria and nephrolithiasis
  • Thiazide diuretics to ↓ urinary calcium (up to 100 mg/d in hypoparathyroid patients)
  • PTH(1-84) (natpara): FDA-approved as adjunct for hypoparathyroidism refractory to calcium/vitamin D

topics.title: Pseudohypoparathyroidism
topics.content:
Classification
TypeHypocalcemiaUrinary cAMP Response to PTHSerum PTHAHO FeaturesGsα Deficiency
PHP1AYes↓↑YesYes
PPHPNoNormalNormalYesYes
PHP1BYes↓↑No (usually)No
PHP2YesNormal↑NoNo
Key Concepts
  • PHP1A: Inactivating mutations in GNAS (encodes Gsα) inherited from mother → PTH resistance + resistance to TSH, gonadotropins; Albright's hereditary osteodystrophy (AHO): short stature, round face, obesity, brachydactyly, subcutaneous calcifications
  • PPHP: Same GNAS mutation inherited from father → AHO features but NO hormone resistance (imprinting effect — Gsα expressed from maternal allele in renal tubules)
  • PHP1B: Epigenetic defects at GNAS locus → PTH resistance without AHO features
  • PHP2: Normal urinary cAMP response but impaired phosphaturic response to PTH

topics.title: Overview — Heme Biosynthesis Pathway
topics.content:
Heme is synthesized through an 8-step enzymatic pathway. The first and last three steps occur in mitochondria; the intermediate four steps occur in the cytosol. Each porphyria results from a deficiency in one of these enzymes (except ALA synthase, where a gain-of-function causes X-linked protoporphyria).

Pathway (Enzyme → Substrate/Product → Associated Porphyria)
StepEnzymePorphyria if Deficient
1ALA synthase 2 (ALAS2)X-linked sideroblastic anemia (loss-of-function); X-linked protoporphyria (XLP) (gain-of-function)
2ALA dehydratase (ALAD)ALAD-deficient porphyria (ADP)
3PBG deaminase (HMBS)Acute intermittent porphyria (AIP)
4Uroporphyrinogen III synthaseCongenital erythropoietic porphyria (CEP)
5Uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase (UROD)Porphyria cutanea tarda (PCT)
6Coproporphyrinogen oxidase (CPOX)Hereditary coproporphyria (HCP)
7Protoporphyrinogen oxidase (PPOX)Variegate porphyria (VP)
8Ferrochelatase (FECH)Erythropoietic protoporphyria (EPP)
Key substrate: Glycine + Succinyl-CoA → δ-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) — the rate-limiting step, catalyzed by ALAS.

topics.title: Classification
topics.content:
By Primary Site of Overproduction
  • Hepatic porphyrias: AIP, ADP, PCT, HCP, VP (enzyme deficiency primarily in the liver)
  • Erythropoietic porphyrias: CEP, EPP, XLP (enzyme deficiency primarily in erythroid cells)
By Clinical Presentation
  • Acute (neurovisceral) porphyrias: AIP, ADP, HCP, VP — characterized by acute attacks with abdominal pain, neuropsychiatric symptoms, autonomic dysfunction
  • Cutaneous porphyrias: PCT, CEP, EPP, XLP, HCP, VP — characterized by photosensitivity (blistering or non-blistering)
  • Mixed (acute + cutaneous): HCP, VP

topics.title: Acute Intermittent Porphyria (AIP) — Most Common Acute Porphyria
topics.content:
Genetics and Pathophysiology
  • Autosomal dominant; mutations in HMBS gene (PBG deaminase / hydroxymethylbilane synthase)
  • ~50% reduction in PBG deaminase activity
  • Low clinical penetrance (~10-20% of carriers develop symptoms)
  • Attacks triggered by factors that induce hepatic ALAS1: drugs (barbiturates, sulfonamides, anticonvulsants, estrogens, rifampin), fasting/caloric restriction, alcohol, infection, stress, menstrual cycle (luteal phase)
Clinical Features — Acute Attack
  • Abdominal pain (most common, >90%): severe, colicky, poorly localized, often mimics surgical abdomen
  • Neuropsychiatric: anxiety, insomnia, depression, confusion, hallucinations, seizures
  • Autonomic dysfunction: tachycardia, hypertension, sweating, constipation, nausea/vomiting, urinary retention
  • Motor neuropathy: progressive weakness (can → respiratory failure), primarily motor (resembles Guillain-Barré but with proximal > distal pattern)
  • Hyponatremia (SIADH) — common and clinically significant
  • Dark/reddish urine (due to PBG oxidizing to porphobilin on standing)
Diagnosis
  • Urine PBG (porphobilinogen): ↑↑↑ during attack (>10× normal) — the single most important screening test for acute porphyria
  • Urine ALA: ↑↑↑
  • Urine porphyrins: ↑ (mainly uroporphyrin)
  • Erythrocyte PBG deaminase: ↓ (~50% of normal) — confirms AIP but normal in ~5% of AIP patients (non-erythroid variant)
  • Fecal porphyrins: Normal in AIP (distinguishes from HCP and VP)
  • Between attacks: PBG may be normal or only mildly elevated
Treatment of Acute Attack
  1. IV Hemin (hematin/heme arginate): Treatment of choice — replenishes hepatic heme pool, suppresses ALAS1 via negative feedback; give as early as possible
  2. IV Glucose (10% dextrose, ≥300 g/d): For mild attacks or if hemin unavailable — suppresses ALAS1 but less effective than hemin
  3. Givosiran (RNAi targeting ALAS1 mRNA): FDA-approved for recurrent acute attacks; monthly subcutaneous injection; reduces ALA and PBG levels
  4. Supportive care: Opioids for pain (safe), ondansetron for nausea, beta-blockers for tachycardia/HTN, correct hyponatremia carefully
  5. Avoid seizure triggers: If seizures occur — gabapentin, levetiracetam, or magnesium are safe; AVOID phenytoin, carbamazepine, barbiturates, valproate
Prevention
  • Avoid known precipitants (check drug safety at porphyria drug databases)
  • Adequate caloric intake, avoid fasting
  • GnRH analogs for women with catamenial attacks
  • Givosiran for recurrent attacks
Long-Term Complications
  • Hepatocellular carcinoma: Annual liver imaging recommended for all AIP patients >50 years
  • Chronic kidney disease: Monitor renal function
  • Hypertension: Requires long-term monitoring

topics.title: Porphyria Cutanea Tarda (PCT) — Most Common Porphyria Overall
topics.content:
Genetics and Pathophysiology
  • Deficiency of uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase (UROD) in the liver
  • Type I (sporadic, ~75%): Acquired; UROD deficiency only in the liver (no gene mutation)
  • Type II (familial, ~25%): Autosomal dominant; UROD mutation → ~50% enzyme activity in all tissues
  • Both types require additional factors to trigger clinical disease: iron overload is the key precipitant
  • Susceptibility factors: HFE mutations (hemochromatosis), hepatitis C, HIV, alcohol, estrogens, smoking
Clinical Features
  • Skin fragility and blistering on sun-exposed areas (dorsum of hands, face, forearms)
  • Vesicles and bullae → erosions → crusting → scarring
  • Hypertrichosis (face), hyperpigmentation, sclerodermoid changes
  • Occurs in adults (not childhood)
  • No acute neurovisceral attacks (distinguishes from VP and HCP)
Diagnosis
  • Plasma porphyrins: ↑↑ (characteristic fluorescence emission peak at ~619 nm)
  • Urine porphyrins: ↑↑ (predominantly uroporphyrin and heptacarboxyl porphyrin — isocoproporphyrin pattern)
  • Urine ALA/PBG: Normal or only slightly elevated (key distinction from acute porphyrias)
  • Fecal porphyrins: ↑ (isocoproporphyrins — specific for PCT)
  • Liver iron: Usually elevated
  • Check for HFE mutations, hepatitis C, HIV
Treatment
  1. Phlebotomy: First-line — serial removal of 450 mL blood every 1-2 weeks until ferritin reaches lower limit of normal (~20 ng/mL); remission typically in 6-12 months
  2. Low-dose hydroxychloroquine (100 mg twice weekly): Alternative if phlebotomy not tolerated — mobilizes hepatic porphyrins; full dose causes hepatotoxicity
  3. Treat underlying hepatitis C (direct-acting antivirals)
  4. Eliminate alcohol, estrogens, iron supplements
  5. Sun protection

topics.title: Hereditary Coproporphyria
topics.content:
HCP
  • Autosomal dominant; deficiency of coproporphyrinogen oxidase (CPOX)
  • Both acute neurovisceral attacks AND cutaneous photosensitivity (blistering)
  • Diagnosis: ↑ urine ALA/PBG during attacks; ↑↑ fecal coproporphyrin III (key diagnostic finding)
  • Treatment: Same as AIP for acute attacks
VP
  • Autosomal dominant; deficiency of protoporphyrinogen oxidase (PPOX)
  • Most common in South Africa (founder effect in Afrikaners, ~1:300)
  • Both acute attacks and cutaneous blistering (identical to PCT skin lesions)
  • Diagnosis: ↑ urine ALA/PBG during attacks; ↑↑ fecal protoporphyrin and coproporphyrin; plasma fluorescence emission peak at ~626 nm (distinguishes VP from PCT ~619 nm)
  • Treatment: Same as AIP for acute attacks; sun protection for skin disease

topics.title: Erythropoietic Protoporphyria
topics.content:
EPP
  • Autosomal recessive; deficiency of ferrochelatase (FECH), the last enzyme in heme biosynthesis
  • Most common erythropoietic porphyria in children; second most common porphyria in adults after PCT
  • FECH activity: 15-25% of normal in symptomatic patients
XLP
  • X-linked; gain-of-function mutations in ALAS2 → increased protoporphyrin IX production
  • ~2-10% of patients with EPP phenotype; higher protoporphyrin levels than EPP
Clinical Features (Both)
  • Acute painful photosensitivity — differs from other cutaneous porphyrias:
    • Begins in early childhood on first sun exposure
    • Tingling, stinging, burning within minutes of sun exposure
    • Redness and swelling but NO blistering (unlike PCT/VP/HCP)
    • Pain out of proportion to visible skin changes
    • Lichenification, labial grooving, nail changes in chronic disease
  • Hepatic complications: Protoporphyrin IX accumulates in liver → cholestatic liver disease → can progress to liver failure requiring transplantation (5-20% develop significant liver disease); gallstones (protoporphyrin stones)
Diagnosis
  • ↑↑ Erythrocyte protoporphyrin IX: Free (not zinc-complexed) — hallmark
  • Distinguish EPP vs XLP: Proportion of free vs zinc protoporphyrin (EPP: predominantly free; XLP: up to 50% zinc-complexed)
  • Urine porphyrins: Normal (protoporphyrin is not water-soluble — excreted in bile/feces)
  • Fecal protoporphyrin: ↑
  • Erythrocytes show red fluorescence at 620 nm
  • FECH activity in lymphocytes/fibroblasts: ↓ in EPP
  • DNA analysis: FECH mutations in EPP; ALAS2 mutations in XLP
Treatment
  • Strict sun avoidance and protective clothing (window glass does NOT protect — protoporphyrin absorbs visible light 400-410 nm, Soret band)
  • Afamelanotide (α-MSH analogue): Subcutaneous implant, FDA-approved; stimulates melanin production → increased sun tolerance
  • Monitor liver function closely; serum ferritin, protoporphyrin levels
  • Hepatic protoporphyrin accumulation: IV hematin, plasmapheresis, cholestyramine may help
  • Liver transplantation for end-stage liver disease (but disease can recur in transplanted liver due to ongoing bone marrow overproduction)
  • Bone marrow transplantation: Curative (eliminates source of excess protoporphyrin)

topics.title: Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria
topics.content:
• Autosomal recessive; deficiency of uroporphyrinogen III synthase
  • Rarest but most severe cutaneous porphyria
  • Massive overproduction of type I porphyrin isomers (uroporphyrin I, coproporphyrin I) in bone marrow
  • Clinical: Severe photosensitivity from infancy → blistering, scarring, mutilation of face/hands; erythrodontia (red fluorescent teeth under Wood's lamp); hemolytic anemia; splenomegaly
  • Treatment: Sun avoidance, transfusion to suppress erythropoiesis, splenectomy, bone marrow transplantation (curative)

topics.title: Key Diagnostic Approach to Porphyrias
topics.content:
Step 1: Clinical Presentation
  • Acute neurovisceral symptoms → Check urine PBG (spot urine, quantitative)
  • Blistering photosensitivity → Check plasma porphyrins, urine porphyrins
  • Non-blistering photosensitivity → Check erythrocyte protoporphyrin
Step 2: Distinguish Among Acute Porphyrias (if PBG elevated)
FeatureAIPHCPVP
Skin lesionsNoYesYes
Fecal coproporphyrinNormal↑↑↑↑
Fecal protoporphyrinNormalNormal↑↑↑
Plasma fluorescence peak——626 nm
Enzyme defectPBG deaminaseCPOXPPOX
Step 3: Distinguish Among Cutaneous Porphyrias (if blistering)
FeaturePCTVPHCPCEP
Acute attacksNoYesYesNo
Urine uroporphyrin↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑ (type I)
Isocoproporphyrin↑↑↑———
Plasma peak619 nm626 nm—619 nm

topics.title: Sex Development — Overview
topics.content:
Sex development has three components: chromosomal sex (XX or XY), gonadal sex (ovary or testis), and phenotypic sex (external/internal genitalia and secondary sexual characteristics).

Key Genetic Determinants
  • SRY (sex-determining region on Y chromosome): Master switch for testis development; activates SOX9
  • SOX9: Upregulated in developing testis, suppressed in ovary
  • WNT4, RSPO1, FOXL2: Promote ovarian development
  • Developing testis → Sertoli cells (produce AMH → müllerian regression) + Leydig cells (produce testosterone → wolffian development, and DHT → external male genitalia)
Disorders of Sex Development (DSDs) — Classification
CategoryExamples
Sex chromosome DSD47,XXY (Klinefelter), 45,X (Turner), 45,X/46,XY mosaicism
46,XY DSDDisorders of testis development (gonadal dysgenesis), disorders of androgen synthesis (CAH, 5α-reductase deficiency), disorders of androgen action (AIS)
46,XX DSDDisorders of ovarian development, androgen excess (CAH — 21-hydroxylase deficiency), ovotesticular DSD

topics.title: Klinefelter Syndrome
topics.content:
• Most common sex chromosome aneuploidy in males: ~1 in 660 live births
  • 80% are 47,XXY; 20% mosaic (46,XY/47,XXY) or higher aneuploidies (48,XXXY)
  • Most result from meiotic nondisjunction (40% maternal spermatogenesis, 60% paternal oogenesis)
Clinical Features
  • Small, firm testes (median 2.5 cm, always <3.5 cm) — most consistent finding
  • Tall stature, increased leg length (eunuchoid proportions)
  • Gynecomastia (~50%)
  • Azoospermia/infertility (seminiferous tubule hyalinization)
  • Learning difficulties, speech delay, behavioral issues
  • ↑ FSH, ↑ LH, ↓ testosterone (50-75%), ↑ estradiol
  • Increased risk: breast cancer, metabolic syndrome, osteoporosis, thromboembolic disease, autoimmune disorders
Treatment
  • Testosterone replacement from puberty onwards
  • Fertility: Testicular sperm extraction + ICSI (successful in >50% of nonmosaic KS at specialized centers)
  • Monitor: Bone density, metabolic syndrome, breast examination

topics.title: Turner Syndrome
topics.content:
• ~1 in 2500 live female births
  • ~50% have 45,X karyotype; ~20% mosaic 45,X/46,XX; remainder have structural X abnormalities
  • Haploinsufficiency of X chromosomal genes (especially SHOX for short stature)
Clinical Features by Age
  • Infancy: Lymphedema (hands/feet), web neck, shield chest, low-set hairline, coarctation of aorta, horseshoe kidney
  • Childhood: Short stature, cubitus valgus, short 4th metacarpals, multiple nevi, recurrent otitis media
  • Puberty: Absent or incomplete puberty, primary amenorrhea (streak gonads → hypergonadotropic hypogonadism)
  • Adulthood: Infertility, hypertension, impaired glucose tolerance, dyslipidemia, autoimmune thyroid disease, osteoporosis, aortic root dilation
Cardiac Monitoring (Critical)
  • Bicuspid aortic valve (30%), coarctation of aorta (30-50%), aortic root dilation
  • Serial cardiac MRI for aortic dimensions
  • Risk of aortic dissection
Treatment
  • GH therapy for short stature (start in childhood)
  • Estrogen replacement to induce puberty (~age 11-12 years), then cyclic estrogen-progestogen
  • Monitor: Thyroid function, glucose tolerance, hearing, renal function, aortic root dimensions

topics.title: Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH) — 21-Hydroxylase Deficiency
topics.content:
• Most common cause of adrenogenital syndrome; most common cause of ambiguous genitalia in 46,XX newborns
  • Autosomal recessive; mutations in CYP21A2
  • Incidence: Classic form 1 in 10,000-15,000; non-classic 1 in 100-500
Pathophysiology
  • Impaired 21-hydroxylase → ↓ cortisol and ↓ aldosterone synthesis
  • ↑ ACTH (loss of negative feedback) → adrenal hyperplasia
  • Shunting of steroid precursors into androgen pathway → ↑ 17-hydroxyprogesterone (17-OHP), ↑ androgens (DHEA, androstenedione)
Clinical Forms
FeatureClassic Salt-Wasting (75%)Classic Simple Virilizing (25%)Non-Classic (Late-Onset)
Cortisol↓↓↓Normal/low-normal
Aldosterone↓↓Normal/lowNormal
17-OHP↑↑↑↑↑↑↑ (mild)
Salt-wasting crisisYes (5-21 days of life)NoNo
46,XX virilizationAmbiguous genitalia at birthAmbiguous genitalia at birthHirsutism, acne, oligomenorrhea in adolescence/adulthood
46,XY presentationNormal male genitalia; salt-wasting crisisPrecocious pubertyPremature adrenarche, infertility
Diagnosis
  • Newborn screening: Elevated 17-OHP (many countries screen)
  • ACTH stimulation test: Exaggerated 17-OHP response (most useful for non-classic CAH)
  • Genetic testing: CYP21A2 mutations
Treatment
  • Acute salt-wasting crisis: IV fluid resuscitation, IV hydrocortisone, correct hypoglycemia and hyperkalemia
  • Chronic replacement: Glucocorticoids (hydrocortisone in children — 10-15 mg/m²/day in 2-3 divided doses) + mineralocorticoid (fludrocortisone 0.05-0.2 mg/d) + salt supplements in infancy
  • Goal: Suppress ACTH (normalize 17-OHP and androgens) without causing Cushingoid features
  • 46,XX patients: Surgical management of ambiguous genitalia is debated; current guidelines emphasize informed consent and deferral when possible
  • Women with 21-OHD frequently develop polycystic ovaries and subfertility

topics.title: Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome
topics.content:
• X-linked; mutations in the androgen receptor gene
  • 46,XY individuals with testicular tissue but variable resistance to androgens
Complete AIS (CAIS — formerly "Testicular Feminization")
  • Female phenotype, normal breast development, short/blind vagina, no uterus (AMH is normal → müllerian regression)
  • Sparse/absent pubic and axillary hair
  • Testes: Inguinal or intra-abdominal (often discovered as inguinal hernia in infancy)
  • At puberty: Breast development (aromatization of testosterone), primary amenorrhea
  • Lab: ↑ testosterone (male range), ↑ LH, normal/↑ AMH
  • Gonadectomy: Traditionally recommended (GCC risk ~3%), but timing is debated; can be deferred until after pubertal breast development
Partial AIS (Reifenstein Syndrome)
  • Variable: Penoscrotal hypospadias, micropenis, undescended testes, gynecomastia at puberty
  • Gender identity variable

topics.title: 5α-Reductase Type 2 Deficiency
topics.content:
• Autosomal recessive; mutations in SRD5A2
  • 46,XY individuals with impaired conversion of testosterone → DHT
  • Birth: Ambiguous genitalia — micropenis, perineal hypospadias, blind vaginal pouch
  • Puberty: Significant virilization (testosterone-mediated) — voice deepening, muscle development, phallic enlargement, but no male-pattern baldness, minimal prostate growth
  • Normal wolffian structures (testosterone-dependent), absent/reduced DHT-dependent structures
  • Gender role change from female to male at puberty reported in some cohorts

topics.title: Menopause and Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy
topics.content:
Definition and Physiology
  • Menopause: Permanent cessation of menses due to loss of ovarian follicular activity; diagnosed after 12 consecutive months of amenorrhea without other cause
  • Mean age: 51 years (range 45-55)
  • Perimenopause/menopausal transition: Begins with menstrual irregularity, ends 12 months after last period; lasts 2-8 years
  • Hormonal changes: ↓ estradiol, ↓ inhibin B → ↑ FSH (>25 IU/L confirms menopause), ↑ LH
  • Premature ovarian insufficiency (POI): Menopause before age 40
Symptoms
  • Vasomotor symptoms (VMS): Hot flashes and night sweats — most common symptom, affect ~75% of women; duration averages 7.4 years
  • Genitourinary syndrome of menopause (GSM): Vaginal dryness, dyspareunia, urinary urgency/frequency, recurrent UTIs — progressive without treatment
  • Sleep disturbance, mood changes, cognitive complaints
  • Accelerated bone loss: Particularly in first 5 years after menopause
Menopausal Hormone Therapy (MHT/HT)
Indications
  • Moderate-to-severe VMS not responsive to lifestyle modifications
  • Prevention of osteoporosis in women at high risk of fracture who cannot tolerate alternatives
Regimens
  • Estrogen alone: For women with hysterectomy (17β-estradiol oral or transdermal, or conjugated equine estrogens)
  • Estrogen + progestogen: For women with intact uterus (progestogen prevents endometrial hyperplasia/cancer)
  • Transdermal estrogen: Lower risk of VTE and hypertriglyceridemia than oral
Benefits and Risks (Table 395-2 Decision Framework)
RecommendConsider with CautionAvoid
Age <60 years AND menopause onset within 10 years AND low risk of breast cancer/CVDAge ≥60 OR menopause >10 years prior OR moderate risk of breast cancer/CVDHigh risk of breast cancer or CVD OR Age ≥60 with menopause >10 years AND moderate risk
Key Trial Data (Women's Health Initiative)
  • Estrogen + progestogen: ↑ breast cancer (HR 1.26), ↑ coronary events, ↑ stroke, ↑ VTE; ↓ hip fracture, ↓ colorectal cancer
  • Estrogen alone (in hysterectomized women): ↓ breast cancer (RR 0.79), ↓ all-cause mortality in women 50-59; no increase in coronary events
  • "Timing hypothesis": HT initiated closer to menopause (within 10 years) has more favorable cardiovascular profile than when started >10 years post-menopause
Contraindications to Systemic HT
  • Unexplained vaginal bleeding
  • Breast, endometrial, or other estrogen-dependent cancer
  • Active VTE or stroke history
  • Active liver disease
  • Uncontrolled hypertension
Non-Hormonal Alternatives for VMS
  • SSRIs/SNRIs: Venlafaxine, paroxetine (FDA-approved), fluoxetine
  • Gabapentin/pregabalin
  • Clonidine
  • Fezolinetant (NK3 receptor antagonist — FDA-approved for VMS)
GSM-Specific Treatment
  • Low-dose vaginal estrogen (cream, tablets, ring) — minimal systemic absorption
  • Vaginal DHEA (prasterone)
  • Ospemifene (selective estrogen receptor modulator — oral)
Duration of HT
  • Short-term (<5 years for E+P, <7 years for E alone): Appropriate for VMS relief in women without contraindications
  • Long-term (≥5 years E+P, ≥7 years E alone): Requires reassessment — heightened breast cancer risk with E+P
  • Reassess benefits/risks every 6-12 months
  • Consider taper rather than abrupt discontinuation

topics.title: Infertility  — Key Points
topics.content:
Definition
  • Inability to achieve pregnancy after 12 months of unprotected intercourse (6 months if female partner >35 years)
  • Prevalence: ~15% globally
Etiology
  • Female factors (30-40%): Tubal (30-35%), ovulatory dysfunction, uterine factors, endometriosis
  • Male factors (40-50%): Endocrine, anatomic, testicular defects, genetic
  • Unexplained (15-30%): After complete workup
Key Investigations
  • Female: Ovulation assessment (day 21 progesterone), ovarian reserve (day 2-3 FSH, estradiol, AMH, antral follicle count), hysterosalpingogram (HSG), pelvic ultrasound
  • Male: Semen analysis (after 2-7 days abstinence) — oligoasthenozoospermia if sperm <15 million/mL, motility <40%, normal morphology <4%
  • Endocrine workup: TSH, prolactin, androgens
Treatment
