  • Ovulatory dysfunction: Letrozole or clomiphene citrate (first-line for PCOS); gonadotropins if refractory; metformin as adjunct
  • Tubal factor: IVF is treatment of choice
  • Male factor: IUI for mild; IVF with ICSI for severe
  • Unexplained: Ovarian stimulation + IUI → IVF if unsuccessful

topics.title: Erectile Dysfunction  — Key Points
topics.content:
Definition and Epidemiology
  • Inability to achieve/maintain erection sufficient for sexual intercourse
  • Prevalence: 52% of men aged 40-70 (10% complete, 25% moderate, 17% minimal)
  • Risk factors: Diabetes (35-75% prevalence), hypertension, CVD, obesity, smoking, medications
Pathophysiology
  • Erection requires: Nitric oxide (NO) → guanylyl cyclase → ↑ cGMP → smooth muscle relaxation → ↑ blood flow to corpora cavernosa
  • PDE-5 degrades cGMP; PDE-5 inhibitors maintain erection by blocking this degradation
Causes (Remember: Vascular > Neurogenic > Endocrine > Psychogenic)
  • Vasculogenic (most common organic cause): Atherosclerosis, diabetes — ED may be "sentinel symptom" of CVD
  • Neurogenic: Spinal cord injury, MS, diabetic neuropathy, post-prostatectomy
  • Endocrinologic: Hypogonadism, hyperprolactinemia, thyroid disorders
  • Medication-induced (~25% of cases): Antihypertensives (thiazides, beta-blockers), SSRIs (especially paroxetine, escitalopram), antipsychotics, antiandrogens
  • Psychogenic: Performance anxiety, depression, relationship issues
Treatment
  1. PDE-5 inhibitors (first-line): Sildenafil, vardenafil, tadalafil, avanafil
    • Contraindicated with nitrates (severe hypotension)
    • Effective in ~60-70% of men
  2. Intracavernosal injection: Alprostadil (PGE1), papaverine, phentolamine
  3. Vacuum constriction devices
  4. Penile prosthesis: For refractory cases
  5. Treat underlying cause: Testosterone replacement if hypogonadal, dopamine agonist if hyperprolactinemia

topics.title: Uric Acid Metabolism — Overview
topics.content:
• Uric acid: Final breakdown product of purine degradation in humans (humans lack uricase)
  • Normal serum urate: Men 415 μmol/L (6.14 mg/dL); premenopausal women 360 μmol/L (4.87 mg/dL)
  • Plasma saturation: 405 μmol/L (6.8 mg/dL) at 37°C → above this, supersaturation and crystal risk
  • Hyperuricemia: >405 μmol/L (>6.8 mg/dL)
  • Excretion: ~2/3 renal, ~1/3 intestinal
Renal Handling of Uric Acid
  • Key transporters: URAT1 (apical reabsorption — target of uricosuric drugs), OAT1/OAT3 (basolateral secretion), GLUT9/SLC2A9 (basolateral efflux — high-capacity urate transporter)
  • Uricosuric drugs (probenecid, lesinurad, losartan, high-dose salicylates >2 g/d): Block URAT1 on apical side
  • Anti-uricosuric drugs (pyrazinamide, nicotinate, low-dose salicylates, cyclosporine, ethambutol, diuretics): Promote urate reabsorption

topics.title: Causes of Hyperuricemia
topics.content:
Increased Urate Production
  • Primary: Idiopathic, HPRT deficiency (Lesch-Nyhan), PRPP synthetase overactivity
  • Secondary: Myeloproliferative/lymphoproliferative diseases, polycythemia vera, psoriasis, hemolysis, rhabdomyolysis, obesity, high-purine diet, alcohol, exercise, tumor lysis
Decreased Uric Acid Excretion
  • Primary: Idiopathic
  • Secondary: Renal insufficiency, lead nephropathy (saturnine gout), hypertension, metabolic syndrome, hypothyroidism, drugs (diuretics, low-dose salicylates, cyclosporine, pyrazinamide, ethambutol)
Combined
  • Alcohol (↑ production via ATP breakdown + ↓ excretion via lactate competition)
  • Glucose-6-phosphatase deficiency, fructose-1-phosphate aldolase deficiency

topics.title: Hyperuricemia and Metabolic Syndrome
topics.content:
• Hyperuricemia is a component of metabolic syndrome
  • Hyperinsulinemia → ↓ renal uric acid and sodium excretion
  • May precede onset of type 2 diabetes, hypertension, coronary artery disease, and gout

topics.title: Complications of Hyperuricemia
topics.content:
• Gouty arthritis (~3.9% prevalence in US): See Rheumatology for detailed gout management
  • Nephrolithiasis: Uric acid stones (~50% when urate >770 μmol/L); also ↑ calcium oxalate stones
  • Urate nephropathy: Chronic monosodium urate crystal deposition in renal interstitium
  • Uric acid nephropathy: Acute — precipitation in collecting ducts (tumor lysis syndrome)

topics.title: Management of Hyperuricemia
topics.content:
Asymptomatic Hyperuricemia
  • Not routinely treated — most hyperuricemic persons never develop gout
  • Exception: Patients receiving cytolytic therapy (rasburicase or allopurinol for tumor lysis prophylaxis)
  • Correct contributing factors: Obesity, alcohol, medications, metabolic syndrome
Pharmacologic Therapy (for symptomatic gout/stones)
  • Xanthine oxidase inhibitors: Allopurinol (first-line; start 100 mg/d, titrate to target urate <6 mg/dL; dose-reduce in renal impairment — oxypurinol accumulates), febuxostat (40-80 mg/d; no renal dose adjustment needed)
  • Uricosuric agents: Probenecid, lesinurad
  • Rasburicase (recombinant uricase): IV; for tumor lysis syndrome prevention — rapidly reduces urate levels; contraindicated in G6PD deficiency (produces H₂O₂ → hemolysis)
  • Pegloticase (pegylated uricase): For refractory tophaceous gout
Uric Acid Nephropathy (Acute)
  • Vigorous IV hydration + furosemide (urine flow ≥100 mL/h)
  • Alkalinize urine (acetazolamide 240-500 mg q6-8h + sodium bicarbonate) — target urine pH >7.0
  • Allopurinol 8 mg/kg single dose to reduce urate reaching kidney
  • Rasburicase if refractory
  • Hemodialysis may be required

topics.title: Hypouricemia
topics.content:
• Serum urate <120 μmol/L (<2.0 mg/dL)
  • Usually from increased renal uric acid excretion
  • Causes: Loss-of-function URAT1 mutations (SLC22A12 — familial renal hypouricemia), Fanconi syndrome, medications with uricosuric properties (losartan, high-dose salicylates)
  • Usually asymptomatic; rare complications: Exercise-induced renal failure, nephrolithiasis

topics.title: Inborn Errors of Purine Metabolism
topics.content:
Enzyme DefectInheritanceKey Clinical Features
HPRT complete deficiency (Lesch-Nyhan)X-linkedSelf-mutilation, choreoathetosis, spasticity, mental retardation, hyperuricemia, gout, uric acid lithiasis
HPRT partial deficiency (Kelley-Seegmiller)X-linkedHyperuricemia, gout, nephrolithiasis — NO CNS involvement
PRPP synthetase overactivityX-linkedGout, uric acid lithiasis, deafness (some families)
APRT deficiencyAR2,8-dihydroxyadenine kidney stones (allopurinol prevents)
Xanthine oxidase deficiencyARXanthinuria, xanthine lithiasis; hypouricemia
Adenosine deaminase deficiencyARSevere combined immunodeficiency (SCID)
Purine nucleoside phosphorylase deficiencyART cell–mediated immunodeficiency

topics.title: Inborn Errors of Pyrimidine Metabolism
topics.content:
DisorderKey Features
Orotic aciduria (UMP synthase deficiency)Megaloblastic anemia unresponsive to B₁₂/folate, growth retardation, orotic acid crystalluria; Rx: uridine 100-200 mg/kg/d
Pyrimidine 5'-nucleotidase deficiencyHemolytic anemia, basophilic stippling of erythrocytes
Dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase deficiencySeizures, mental retardation; 5-fluorouracil neurotoxicity (this enzyme is rate-limiting for 5-FU degradation)
Drug Interactions with Pyrimidine Metabolism
  • 5-Fluorouracil: Requires conversion to active metabolite by thymidylate synthase pathway; dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase deficiency → severe 5-FU toxicity
  • Leflunomide: Inhibits dihydroorotate dehydrogenase (de novo pyrimidine synthesis) — used for rheumatoid arthritis
  • Allopurinol: Inhibits orotidine-5'-phosphate decarboxylase → ↑ orotidine and orotic acid excretion

Sources: Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine, 21st Edition (Chapters 390, 395-397, 403-405, 409-410, 416-417); ADA Standards of Care 2025 (Sections 2, 4, 7, 11, 16)
